name: Governance – Oversight Structure
lever_classification: governance — who oversees the platform‑neutral advocacy and procurement influence
consequences: Pulling this lever establishes a formal oversight body that can streamline decision‑making and signal commitment to platform neutrality. It may accelerate policy approvals but risks concentrating power and reducing broader stakeholder input. The resulting committee could also become a target for lobbying, potentially diluting its independence.
options: Establish a cross‑ministerial steering committee with representatives from Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, Folketinget, and civil‑society to coordinate advocacy.
Create an independent advisory board of security experts and EU regulators to vet proposals and provide technical credibility.
Form a public‑private partnership that includes major Danish telecom operators to align commercial incentives with platform neutrality.
review: Centralizing oversight can speed decision‑making but risks alienating diverse stakeholders; the options assume the committee will act swiftly, yet they overlook potential bureaucratic inertia that could delay approvals.

name: Methodology – Risk Assessment Approach
lever_classification: methodology — how to assess platform dependency risk
consequences: Adopting a structured risk assessment approach provides quantifiable evidence of platform lock‑in, strengthening the case for fallback requirements. However, the detailed audit and simulation may consume significant time and expertise, delaying the delivery of actionable findings to procurement bodies.
options: Conduct a comprehensive dependency audit using a formal supply‑chain risk matrix that scores each platform signal on criticality and resilience.
Deploy a simulation model that injects failure scenarios into a virtual identity stack to quantify fallback performance.
Perform a stakeholder‑driven Delphi study that aggregates expert judgments on platform lock‑in and fallback feasibility.
review: Choosing a rigorous risk assessment method may yield precise data but could consume resources and delay action; the options ignore the need for rapid evidence to influence procurement timelines.

name: Execution – Demonstrator Sequencing
lever_classification: execution — how to sequence demonstrator development and stakeholder engagement
consequences: Sequencing the mobile demonstrator first creates a tangible proof of concept that can be leveraged in stakeholder meetings, potentially expediting policy discussions. Yet, focusing on one prototype early may leave the browser fallback under‑developed, limiting the breadth of evidence presented to regulators.
options: Build the mobile‑oriented demonstrator first, then use its results to secure early stakeholder buy‑in before launching the browser‑based fallback prototype.
Parallel‑track both demonstrators, allocating separate teams to maintain momentum while sharing findings in joint workshops.
Delay demonstrator development until after a formal policy briefing, ensuring that technical work is directly informed by regulatory requirements.
review: Sequencing demonstrator work before stakeholder engagement may produce compelling evidence but risks misalignment with policy needs; the options assume resources can be split without compromising quality.

name: Dissemination – Policy Communication Strategy
lever_classification: dissemination — how to communicate findings to policymakers and the public
consequences: A targeted policy brief and workshops can elevate the project’s visibility among decision‑makers, fostering support for platform‑neutral language. Nonetheless, without tailored messaging for each political actor, the brief risks being misinterpreted or dismissed as technical jargon.
options: Publish a concise policy brief with executive summaries, data visualizations, and actionable recommendations targeted at Digitaliseringsstyrelsen.
Host a series of round‑table workshops with key stakeholders, including civil‑society, academia, and industry, to co‑create policy language.
Release an open‑source toolkit that demonstrates fallback authentication, accompanied by a media campaign highlighting sovereignty benefits.
review: Broad dissemination can raise awareness but may dilute technical nuance; the options overlook tailoring messaging to different political actors and risk misinterpretation.

name: Product – Fallback Authentication Design
lever_classification: product — design of fallback authentication prototype
consequences: Developing a hardware‑token fallback demonstrates a secure, platform‑independent solution that can be cited in procurement specifications. The approach may, however, face usability challenges for citizens accustomed to mobile‑based authentication, potentially reducing adoption momentum.
options: Develop a hardware‑token fallback that uses a USB‑C or NFC device, ensuring platform‑independence and strong cryptographic guarantees.
Build a browser‑extension fallback that leverages WebAuthn and local credential storage, avoiding mobile OS dependencies.
Create a hybrid mobile‑app fallback that runs on GrapheneOS, using a custom trust‑anchor to bypass platform integrity checks.
review: Hardware‑token fallback offers strong security but may face usability barriers; the options assume end‑user adoption will be high, ignoring friction in everyday use.

name: Operations – Budget Allocation Across Phases
lever_classification: operations — how to allocate resources across project phases
consequences: Front‑loading the budget into phase one ensures robust coalition building and stakeholder alignment before technical work begins. This allocation may, however, leave later phases vulnerable to funding gaps if cost overruns occur or if political priorities shift.
options: Allocate the largest share of the budget to phase one, ensuring robust coalition building before technical work begins.
Distribute funds evenly across all phases, maintaining steady progress and reducing risk of funding gaps.
Concentrate the majority of resources in phase two, prioritizing demonstrator development to generate hard evidence for procurement.
review: Front‑loading budget to early phases can secure coalition but may leave later phases underfunded; the options ignore potential cost overruns in later phases.

name: Governance – Decision-Making Cadence
lever_classification: governance — how often and in what format decisions are reviewed and adjusted
consequences: Pulling this lever changes the frequency and format of decision reviews, accelerating policy adoption but increasing administrative overhead and stakeholder fatigue.
options: Set up a bi‑monthly in‑person steering committee that evaluates progress and adjusts procurement language to respond swiftly to regulatory shifts.
Launch a digital dashboard that aggregates stakeholder feedback and automatically triggers quarterly review meetings, enabling remote participation and cutting travel costs.
Form a rotating advisory panel of civil society, academia, and industry experts that convenes annually, providing fresh perspectives while potentially slowing decision cycles.
review: Balancing swift decision cycles against stakeholder inclusivity; the options assume all key actors can attend scheduled meetings, overlooking the likelihood that senior policymakers may be unavailable during bi‑monthly or annual sessions.

name: Methodology – Evidence Generation
lever_classification: methodology — how evidence is collected and validated to support policy proposals
consequences: Pulling this lever changes how evidence is gathered, affecting credibility of policy proposals; rigorous evidence may delay proposals but increase acceptance, while less rigorous evidence may speed but risk rejection.
options: Carry out a comparative security audit of existing MitID platform integrity signals against open‑source alternatives, documenting gaps and mitigation strategies.
Execute a stakeholder‑driven scenario analysis that models potential supply‑chain disruptions and quantifies resilience benefits of platform neutrality.
Assemble a cross‑disciplinary research team to publish a peer‑reviewed white paper on fallback authentication feasibility, leveraging academic credibility.
review: Ensuring evidence rigor versus speed; the options assume internal audits suffice, neglecting the requirement for independent third‑party validation to satisfy procurement reviewers.

name: Execution – Coalition Mobilization
lever_classification: execution — how coalition partners are engaged and coordinated to influence procurement decisions
consequences: Pulling this lever changes how coalition partners are engaged, affecting influence on procurement; a stronger coalition may increase political pressure but risk fragmentation, while a weaker coalition may be more cohesive but less influential.
options: Host a national roundtable that brings together civil‑society NGOs, privacy advocates, and academic experts to co‑draft a unified platform‑neutrality brief.
Initiate a targeted lobbying campaign that enlists key parliamentary members to sponsor a formal amendment to the AltID procurement specification.
Develop a digital collaboration portal that allows distributed stakeholders to contribute proposals, vote on priorities, and track progress in real time.
review: Balancing breadth versus cohesion; the options assume all invited parties will align, overlooking potential conflicting interests between NGOs, academia, and industry stakeholders.

name: Dissemination – Narrative Framing
lever_classification: dissemination — how the project’s story is framed to shape stakeholder perception and political will
consequences: Pulling this lever changes how the project story is communicated, affecting public and political perception; a compelling narrative may accelerate policy adoption but risk oversimplification, while a technical narrative may be accurate but less persuasive.
options: Create a human‑centered case study that follows a citizen’s journey through digital services, highlighting risks of platform lock‑in and benefits of fallback access.
Develop a data‑rich infographic series that visualizes supplier concentration metrics, resilience scores, and projected service‑continuity scenarios.
Organize a series of moderated panel discussions featuring policymakers, technologists, and affected users to debate the trade‑offs of platform neutrality.
review: Framing risk versus opportunity; the options assume a single narrative will resonate, neglecting that different stakeholder groups prioritize distinct concerns such as privacy, cost, or technical feasibility.

name: Product – Technical Proof of Concept
lever_classification: product — how technical proof of concept is developed to validate fallback authentication viability
consequences: Pulling this lever changes the technical demonstration focus, affecting credibility with regulators; a robust proof may convince procurement but require more resources, while a lightweight demo may be quicker but less convincing.
options: Construct a minimal‑viable FIDO2/WebAuthn authenticator that operates on GrapheneOS, demonstrating platform‑neutral credential issuance without relying on Apple or Google APIs.
Create a browser‑based fallback flow that uses WebAuthn and a server‑side challenge‑response protocol, proving that critical services can be accessed without mobile OS integrity checks.
Develop a hardware‑token prototype that integrates with existing Danish e‑ID cards, demonstrating that physical fallback devices can be issued and validated independently of mobile platforms.
review: Demonstrating feasibility versus scalability; the options focus on proof of concept but overlook the need to assess long‑term operational costs and user adoption challenges.

name: Operations – Resource Diversification
lever_classification: operations — how human and external resources are diversified to mitigate staff turnover and expertise gaps
consequences: Pulling this lever changes how resources are allocated across expertise, affecting resilience to staff turnover; diversified staffing may reduce risk of single point of failure but increase coordination overhead, while concentrated staffing may streamline but risk loss of knowledge.
options: Hire two independent security analysts from separate universities to co‑lead the mobile security component, ensuring knowledge redundancy.
Engage a third‑party legal advisory firm to manage procurement compliance, freeing internal staff to concentrate on technical development.
Allocate a portion of the budget to a shared services pool that supplies cross‑project expertise in EU digital identity standards, reducing duplication across Danish initiatives.
review: Balancing redundancy versus efficiency; the options assume additional hires will integrate smoothly, overlooking potential cultural clashes and onboarding delays that could slow progress.

name: Governance – Legislative Alignment Path
lever_classification: governance — how legislative support is secured
consequences: Pulling this lever ensures alignment with upcoming digital legislation, enabling smoother procurement. It may delay the project timeline due to legislative drafting cycles, but it secures a policy foundation for platform neutrality.
options: Engage parliamentary committees early to embed platform-neutral clauses in draft bills, ensuring legislative alignment and clarity.
Sponsor a cross-party working group to draft model legislation for digital identity resilience, fostering bipartisan support.
Leverage existing EU directives to pressure national lawmakers to adopt fallback requirements, aligning with continental standards.
review: Core tension: balancing rapid procurement with legislative inertia; options overlook the risk of political backlash if platform-neutral clauses are perceived as limiting commercial innovation.

name: Methodology – Technical Feasibility Mapping
lever_classification: methodology — how technical viability is assessed
consequences: Pulling this lever clarifies technical constraints of fallback paths, guiding design choices. It may reveal unforeseen integration hurdles with existing eIDAS infrastructure, requiring additional engineering effort.
options: Conduct a layered threat model mapping platform integrity dependencies across all citizen-facing services, revealing hidden risk vectors.
Perform a comparative architecture analysis between GrapheneOS and standard Android to quantify trust gaps and integration costs.
Simulate fallback authentication flows under varied network conditions to identify latency thresholds and resilience limits.
review: Core tension: technical depth versus stakeholder comprehension; options miss the challenge of translating complex threat models into actionable procurement language.

name: Execution – Stakeholder Negotiation Tactics
lever_classification: execution — how coalition agreements are secured
consequences: Pulling this lever shapes coalition dynamics, ensuring sustained advocacy momentum. It may create intra-coalition conflicts over resource allocation, requiring careful conflict resolution.
options: Facilitate a quarterly roundtable where each partner presents progress and negotiates shared milestones, fostering transparency.
Deploy a digital collaboration platform that tracks contribution metrics and automates recognition tokens, incentivizing participation.
Introduce a rotating leadership model that assigns coalition chairmanship to a different stakeholder each cycle, preventing dominance.
review: Core tension: balancing coalition cohesion with individual stakeholder agendas; options overlook the risk of diluted accountability when leadership rotates.

name: Dissemination – Evidence Publication Strategy
lever_classification: dissemination — how findings are communicated to decision makers
consequences: Pulling this lever ensures evidence reaches procurement officials, influencing policy language. It may increase scrutiny of technical claims by regulators, requiring rigorous validation.
options: Publish a peer-reviewed white paper detailing fallback feasibility, citing empirical performance data and risk mitigation strategies.
Host a live demonstration webinar for ministry officials, showcasing demonstrator capabilities in real time and answering queries.
Release a concise policy brief that translates technical results into procurement requirements, distributed via official channels.
review: Core tension: technical depth versus policy clarity; options miss the challenge of ensuring evidence is digestible for non-technical stakeholders.

name: Product – Interoperability Integration Blueprint
lever_classification: product — how fallback solutions integrate with existing identity services
consequences: Pulling this lever defines integration pathways, reducing future migration friction. It may require additional certification steps for new components, extending the timeline.
options: Design a modular adapter that translates FIDO2 assertions into eIDAS-compliant assertions for legacy services, ensuring compatibility.
Build a gateway service that aggregates fallback authentication tokens and forwards them to existing identity providers, simplifying integration.
Prototype a cross-platform SDK that enables third-party apps to invoke fallback authentication without platform-specific code, boosting adoption.
review: Core tension: ensuring backward compatibility while innovating; options overlook the risk of certification delays for new adapters.

name: Operations – Contingency Funding Model
lever_classification: operations — how risk reserves are allocated
consequences: Pulling this lever secures financial buffers for unexpected delays, preserving project continuity. It may reduce available funds for core deliverables if contingency is overestimated.
options: Allocate a fixed 15% of each phase budget to a contingency pool, monitored by an independent auditor, ensuring fiscal safety.
Establish a rolling reserve that replenishes when milestones are met, ensuring funds remain available for overruns.
Create a joint funding agreement with partner institutions to cover unforeseen technical or regulatory setbacks, sharing risk.
review: Core tension: balancing risk coverage with resource scarcity; options miss the possibility of over-allocating contingency at the expense of core milestones.
